questions: Identify the 8-bit handheld game console released in 1989 that utilized the 'block-stacking' game Tetris as a pack-in title to dominate the market against technically superior competitors like the Atari Lynx. rounds.question: Identify the 8-bit handheld game console released in 1989 that utilized the 'block-stacking' game Tetris as a pack-in title to dominate the market against technically superior competitors like the Atari Lynx. Based on hardware histories describing this product line, specifically compare the original 1989 model to its 1996 'Pocket' revision in terms of power source requirements (battery size and quantity) and the specific visual characteristics of their monochrome displays.
checklist_width: Original Model Power: Required four 'AA' batteries
Revision Model Power: Switched to smaller 'AAA' batteries (and required fewer of them)
Original Model Display: Featured a green-tinted monochrome display
Revision Model Display: Improved to show 'true' black and white graphics (and was slightly bigger)
checklist_depth: Target Entity: Nintendo Game Boy (identified via 1989 release, Tetris, and Lynx rivalry)
Target Revision: Game Boy Pocket (identified via 1996 release date)

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Home » Headline , Retrogaming , Technology Game Boy — Nintendo dominates the portable market By: Ethan C. Nobles 22 January 2011 One Comment Kirby was popular on the Super Nin
rounds.answer_a:
**The Nintendo Game Boy**, an 8-bit handheld console released in 1989, bundled **Tetris** as its pack-in title and dominated rivals like the color-capable Atari Lynx through affordability and gameplay appeal despite inferior specs.[3][4]

The original "DMG-01" model measured 90mm x 148mm x 32mm, weighed about 300g with batteries, and powered via **4 AA batteries** for roughly 15 hours of play.[2][4][5] Its **monochrome display** was a 2.6-inch (66mm) reflective STN dot-matrix LCD at 160x144 resolution, rendering **4 shades of gray/green** with notable ghosting and a greenish hue, requiring external light and contrast adjustment.[1][2][3][5]

The 1996 **Game Boy Pocket** revision shrank to half the size/weight for portability, switching to **2 AAA batteries** yielding about 10 hours of life.[2][4] Its **monochrome display** retained 160x144 resolution and 4 shades but upgraded to a sharper STN LCD with improved pixel response (reducing ghosting), a film compensation layer for **true black-and-white** (no green tint), and better visibility.[3][4]

These changes made the Pocket more efficient and user-friendly while preserving compatibility, cementing the line's market lead.[2][3]

(Word count: 248)

## References

[2] https://www.nintendo.com/en-za/Hardware/Nintendo-History/Game-Boy/Game-Boy-627031.html  
[3] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Game_Boy  
[4] https://gametrog.com/nintendo-game-boy-information-specs/  
[5] https://www.dimensions.com/element/game-boy
